VMI Suite — User Guide

Vehicle–Motor Integration Suite

VMI Suite — User Guide & Reference Manual

A desktop powertrain assessment tool for two- and three-wheeler electric vehicles: motor sizing, drive-cycle analysis, efficiency and range prediction, PMSM control envelopes, mechanical design checks, and BOM cost/weight engineering — in one workbench.

Document VMI-UG-001 Revision 1.0 · July 2026 Applies to VMI Suite (desktop, Windows) Audience First-time users · Powertrain engineers

1What the tool does

The VMI Suite answers the questions a powertrain engineer asks when matching an electric motor (or, for comparison, an IC engine) to a vehicle:

  • Will it move the vehicle? — torque and force at the wheel against rolling, aerodynamic and gradient resistance, at every speed and grade.
  • How fast, how quickly, how steep? — top speed, 0-to-target acceleration time, and maximum gradability, including sensitivity sweeps over CdA and Crr.
  • How efficiently, and how far? — drive-cycle energy with real motor and controller efficiency maps, and a full battery model producing Wh/km and range.
  • How is the motor controlled and built? — MTPA/MTPV operating envelopes for PMSMs, and hand-calculation mechanical design checks (rotor stress, shaft, press fit, bearings, critical speed) to recognised standards.
  • What does it cost and weigh? — a bill-of-materials tree with Sankey, Pareto and A-vs-B comparison views.

Everything runs locally on your PC. Data comes in as Excel and JSON; results go out as figures, Excel exports, saved scenarios, and a multi-analysis HTML report.

2Getting started

  1. Install Python (3.10+) and the dependencies: pip install -r requirements.txt
  2. Start the application: python main.py
  3. Generate ready-made practice data (recommended for a first session): python generate_sample_data.py writes one correctly formatted Excel file per upload slot into sample_data/ — a drive cycle, a motor torque curve, an engine curve, gear-efficiency sheets, two efficiency maps, and three MTPA saturation maps.
  4. Pick an Analysis Type from the dropdown at the top of the left panel, fill in (or keep) the inputs, and press Update Plot — or simply press Enter in any input field.
Every figure in this guide is real output — generated by the application itself from the sample_data/ files and the default vehicle (180 kg reference mass, 0.266 m wheel, 180 Nm / 2.4 kW hub motor). Reproduce any of them by loading the same files and pressing Update Plot.

3The workbench at a glance

The window has three vertical panes:

PaneWhat lives there
Left — InputsThe Analysis Type selector, a ✔/✖ data checklist showing which uploads the selected analysis needs, and collapsible input sections (Vehicle Parameters, Motor Performance Parameters, Simulation Settings, …). Only the sections relevant to the selected analysis are shown; click a section header (▼/▶) to fold it.
Centre — PlotThe figure canvas, the toolbar (export figure/data, Report, scenarios, dark-plot toggle, Assistant), and a status bar that reports progress and input errors. Invalid fields are outlined in red.
Right — Assistant optionalA local chat sidebar (via Ollama) that answers powertrain questions from your own knowledge_base/ documents with per-fact source citations. Fully offline; see §7.
Blank means automatic. Several fields are auto-computed until you type in them: Crr and CdA (estimated from the reference-mass class), and all axis limits. Once you edit such a field manually the tool respects your value and stops overwriting it. Clear the field to hand control back.

4Theory the tool is built on

4.1 Road load — the resistance the motor must beat

At vehicle speed v on a grade of angle θ (entered as % gradient, θ = atan(grade/100)), the resistive force at the wheel is:

Road-load equation Fres = m·g·Crr·cos θ  +  ½·ρ·CdA·v²  +  m·g·sin θ

— rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag (ρ = 1.225 kg/m³ at sea level, optionally altitude-corrected), and grade force. When Crr / CdA are left blank the tool estimates both from a calibrated mass-class lookup, corrected for ambient temperature and pressure.

4.2 The motor capability curve

An electric motor delivers constant peak torque up to its base speed, then constant peak power beyond it:

Capability envelope T(ω) = min( Tpeak ,  Ppeak/ω )   with   ωbase = Ppeak/Tpeak

Wheel quantities follow from the drivetrain: Twheel = Tmotor·G·ηgear and Fwheel = Twheel/r, with gear ratio G, gear efficiency ηgear, and wheel radius r. Instead of the theoretical envelope you can upload a measured torque–speed curve from Excel; every analysis then uses it automatically.

4.3 Battery DC limit optional

A real battery cannot deliver unlimited current. If you enter a battery voltage and a DC current limit, the shaft power available from the motor is capped at

Battery-limited shaft power Pcap = Vdc · Idc,max · ηbattery→shaft   ⇒   T ≤ Pcap/ω

and every capability curve in the tool — torque, force, acceleration, parametric sweeps, motor comparisons, efficiency-map envelope — is clipped accordingly. Leave the fields blank for no limit. Section 5.1 shows the effect.

4.4 Wheel inertia optional

Spinning wheels store rotational energy, so they resist acceleration like extra mass — without adding weight to rolling or grade resistance. The tool applies the translational-equivalent mass to inertial (m·a) terms only:

Effective inertial mass meff = m + Jwheels/r²

Steady-state results (top speed, gradability) are physically unaffected; acceleration times and drive-cycle inertial torque increase with J.

4.5 Efficiency maps, energy and range

A motor (or controller) efficiency map is a matrix η(T, rpm). Combined powertrain efficiency is the product ηmotor·ηcontroller. Over a drive cycle sampled at points (vi, ti), each point's demanded torque follows from the road load plus meff·a; mechanical energy is T·ω·Δt; battery energy while motoring is mechanical energy ÷ combined η, and braking points return energy (regen) multiplied by combined η, optionally capped. Range follows from the usable pack energy divided by net Wh/km.

5The eleven analyses

Each analysis below states its purpose, the inputs that matter most, the procedure, and how to read the output. Shared inputs (mass, wheel, gear, motor ratings, environment) are described once in §5.1 and not repeated.

5.1Powertrain Sizing — torque & force at the wheel

Question it answers: can this motor move this vehicle at every speed and grade — and where does it run out?

Key inputs

FieldUnitMeaning
Reference MasskgVehicle + payload; also selects the mass class used to auto-estimate Crr and CdA when those are blank.
Wheel Radius / Tyre specmDynamic rolling radius. Pick a tyre designation (e.g. 90/90-12) to fill it automatically × the Dynamic Radius Factor.
Gear ratio / Gear efficiency— / 0–1Motor-to-wheel reduction and its efficiency (1 = direct drive hub motor).
Peak Torque / Peak PowerNm / kWMotor ratings defining the theoretical envelope (§4.2). Ignored when a measured curve is uploaded.
Continuous Power, Peak:Rated ratiokW / —Define the continuous (thermal) torque curve plotted alongside the peak curve.
Gradients%Comma-separated list; one resistive curve is drawn per gradient.
Battery Voltage / DC Current LimitV / AOptional battery cap (§4.3); blank = unlimited.
Wheel Inertia Jkg·m²Optional (§4.4); 0 = ignored.

Procedure

  1. Select Powertrain Sizing; choose Output = Torque or Force.
  2. Choose the x-axis in Plot Mode: Speed Unit (km/h or RPM) and Plotting Part (At Wheel / At Motor). Force always plots wheel force.
  3. Optionally upload a measured motor curve (Motor Data → Excel); the plot refreshes automatically.
  4. Press Update Plot.

5.2Acceleration — speed vs time

Question it answers: how long to reach a target speed under full throttle?

The tool integrates a = (Fwheel,max − Fres)/meff from rest at 1 ms steps, using the (battery-capped) capability curve and the first gradient in the Gradients list. Set Target Speed and Max Simulation Time in Simulation Settings.

5.3Parametric Study — CdA / Crr sensitivity

Question it answers: how much does aero or rolling resistance actually matter for this vehicle?

Nine sweep types (choose in the Parametric section): the effect of CdA, Crr, or both on Top Speed, Acceleration Time, and Max Gradability. Enter ranges as min,max,step. Single-parameter sweeps draw a line with a marker showing where your current vehicle sits; two-parameter sweeps draw a filled contour map.

5.4Drive Cycle — demand over a real ride

Question it answers: what does a real usage cycle actually ask of the motor?

Upload a time–speed trace (§6). The tool differentiates it, applies the road-load model plus meff·a, and shows three views: the cycle itself, every operating point in the torque–speed plane, and a binned heatmap of where the cycle spends its time or energy.

5.5Drive Cycle Efficiency — motor × controller maps

Question it answers: how efficient is the powertrain over the cycle, and where is efficiency being lost?

Upload up to two efficiency maps — slot 1 = Motor, slot 2 = Controller (§6). Five map views (buttons in the section): Motor, Controller, Combined (ηm·ηc), Difference (Controller − Motor), and Regen (the motoring map mirrored to braking torque). Cycle metrics are computed two ways: energy-weighted and unweighted average of per-point efficiency, motoring and regen included.

The envelope always wins. Every map is clipped to what the motor can physically reach — peak torque to base speed, then P/ω (battery-capped if set) — and the black capability curve is drawn on top so you can verify nothing is coloured beyond it. Cells the datasheet left blank stay blank (an optional Graph-Settings toggle can neighbour-fill gaps for display).

5.6Compare Standard Motor Data

Question it answers: how do candidate motors from your library stack up on this vehicle?

Save any configured motor to the library (name, curve, gear ratio, wheel radius, and — if loaded — its efficiency map). Then select several and compare four ways (radio buttons): Torque (wheel or motor side, each motor with its own gradient-intersection markers), Force, Acceleration (a full speed–time run per motor), and Efficiency Map (saved motor's map minus the currently loaded one, as a difference map). The battery DC limit, if set, caps every compared motor — the battery belongs to the vehicle.

5.7Engine analysis — IC benchmark

Question it answers: what does the incumbent engine + gearbox deliver at the wheel, gear by gear?

Upload an engine torque–RPM curve and up to six gear ratios (plus optional per-gear efficiency curves from a multi-sheet Excel). The tool draws wheel torque or wheel force per gear over vehicle speed — the sawtooth to beat. One click syncs the engine curve into the motor inputs so every other analysis can run against it.

5.8Range analysis — battery model & Wh/km

Question it answers: how far does it go on this pack, on this cycle — and where does the energy go?

Combines the drive cycle, the efficiency maps (or constant efficiencies), and a battery pack model: cells series × parallel, capacity, voltage, cell efficiency, depth of discharge, auxiliary load, regen cap, integration method. Nine views via one dropdown — All (dashboard), Power, Energy, C-rate, Loss, Waterfall, Drive, and the two maps.

5.9MTPA / MTPV (PMSM) — control-envelope analysis

Question it answers: what can a given PMSM electromagnetic design deliver under proper current/voltage-limited control?

From d-q machine parameters — pole pairs, Ld, Lq, PM flux ψPM, current limit and DC-link voltage — the solver computes the maximum-torque-per-ampere trajectory, the flux-weakening region, and the maximum-torque-per-volt boundary (stator resistance neglected). The current spec accepts Line/Phase × RMS/Peak × Star/Delta; the voltage limit maps Vdc through the selected PWM scheme (SVPWM Vdc/√3, sine Vdc/2, six-step 2Vdc/π). Optional Excel saturation maps for Ld, Lq, ψPM over an (id × iq) grid switch the solver to a saturation-aware search. Four views: torque–speed envelope, power, d-q current trajectories, or All.

5.10Mechanical Design (Motor) — standards-based checks

Question it answers: does the rotor, shaft, fit, bearing and dynamics design hold up — with the safety factors the handbooks demand?

Five hand-calculation design checks, selected from one Design Check dropdown; each swaps in its own input set and produces one plot plus a numeric verdict quoting the target safety factor:

  • Rotor Stress & Burst Speed — spinning-disc stresses, burst margin (Roark/Timoshenko).
  • Shaft Design — static and fatigue (Shigley's DE-Goodman).
  • Press / Shrink Fit — interface pressure vs speed, loss-of-contact RPM (DIN 7190).
  • Bearing Life (L10) — life vs load (ISO 281).
  • Critical Speed & Balancing — first bending mode vs diameter, balance grade (ISO 21940-11).

5.11Motor BOM (Cost & Weight)

Question it answers: where do the rupees and grams actually sit in this motor — and how does architecture B compare to A?

The BOM is a nested assembly tree (quantity × unit cost × unit weight, rolled up from part level). Start from a built-in Mid-Mount or Hub template, import/export flat Excel, or edit in the built-in tree editor. One Metric switch (Cost / Weight) drives all views: Sankey (flow breakdown with small branches folded into "Others"), Pareto (largest contributors with cumulative %), Group Split (by assembly / category / active-flag), and Compare A vs B (load a second tree; one-sided bars for architecture-only assemblies, green/red deltas).

6Data file formats

All uploads are Excel (.xlsx). On upload the tool shows a column/sheet picker, so exact header names don't matter — but the shapes below do. generate_sample_data.py produces a valid example of every format.

DatasetShapeNotes
Drive cycleTwo columns: time (s), speed (km/h) Time strictly increasing; 1 Hz typical.
Motor curveTwo columns: torque (Nm), speed (RPM) Replaces the theoretical envelope everywhere once loaded.
Engine curveTwo columns: torque (Nm), RPM Plus gear ratios entered in the Engine section.
Gear efficiencyOne sheet per gear (G1…G6): RPM, efficiency Optional; interpolated per gear.
Efficiency mapMatrix: first column = torque axis (Nm), header row = RPM axis, body = efficiency (% or 0–1) Slot 1 = Motor, slot 2 = Controller. Blank cells = unmeasured (kept blank). Loading a map auto-fills the motor's Max Torque/Power fields from the populated cells.
MTPA saturation mapsThree files (Ld, Lq, ψPM): first column = id axis (A), header row = iq axis (A), body = value (mH / mH / Wb) Optional; missing maps fall back to the constant entries.
BOM ExcelFlat rows: Assembly | Sub-assembly | Part | Qty | Unit Cost | Unit Weight | Category | Active Header matching is forgiving; values round-trip exactly on export.

7Workflow features

FeatureWhat it does
Session autosaveThe complete UI state — every input, selection and loaded dataset — is saved on close and restored on the next launch. Delete vmi_last_session.json to start clean.
ScenariosSave/Load the same complete state to a named JSON — the unit of "one vehicle variant". Loaded datasets travel inside the file, so a scenario is fully self-contained and shareable.
HTML ReportToolbar → Report opens a checklist of all analyses; the selected ones are rendered into a single self-contained HTML report — inputs table first, then one section per plot view (e.g. Drive Cycle contributes speed-trace, scatter and heatmap) with the numeric summaries.
ExportsSave the current figure as an image; export computed torque-speed data to Excel.
Graph SettingsA per-analysis appearance panel: line colours/styles/ widths, per-axis grid with fixed tick spacing, legend position, font sizes, colormaps and contour levels, heatmap weighting. Untouched settings always reproduce the built-in look.
Dark plot themeToolbar toggle; applied as a post-process, so it works on every plot.
Assistant (local AI)Requires Ollama with llama3.1:8b and nomic-embed-text pulled once. Drop PDFs/Word/Excel into knowledge_base/, click Rebuild Knowledge Base, and ask; answers cite the source file per fact, and clearly flag engineering judgment when your documents don't cover the question. Nothing leaves your machine.

8Tips, conventions & FAQ

  • Red-bordered field? That input didn't parse; the status bar lists every problem at once. Fix and press Enter.
  • Plot didn't change? Press Update Plot (or Enter in a field). Loading a motor curve or changing a Graph Setting replots automatically.
  • Axis looks wrong? You probably typed in an axis-limit field earlier — the tool is honouring your manual limit. Clear the field to return to auto.
  • Torque vs Force? One analysis ("Powertrain Sizing"), two outputs. Force is always at the wheel; the Plotting Part selector locks itself accordingly.
  • Which efficiency map slot is which? Slot 1 = Motor, slot 2 = Controller, everywhere in the tool. Combined = product of the two.
  • Does the battery DC limit change my range numbers? No — it caps capability curves. Drive-cycle demand is demand; if the cycle needs more power than the cap, that shows up in the torque-speed views against the (clipped) envelope.
  • Units: SI throughout — Nm, kW, km/h, RPM, kg, m, %, Wh, kg·m². Currency in the BOM is ₹ per unit.
  • Verified numbers: the physics core is locked by a golden-value regression test suite (python -m pytest tests/, 165 tests) — formulas cannot change silently.
